one_line_takeaway: By probing and ablating reasoning-critical attention heads, the authors show LLM safety refusals are largely reasoning-independent pattern matching, then fix it with a CoT safety dataset plus AW-DPO, a DPO variant that reweights the loss separately for the <think> reasoning span versus the final response span based on per-segment harmfulness-score gaps, cutting average jailbreak ASR to roughly 1-9% across four model families while keeping MMLU utility close to baseline.
key_finding: On Llama-2-7B, average jailbreak Attack Success Rate (ASR, lower is better) drops from 41.32% (base) to 39.71% (+SFT) to 25.99% (+Safety SFT) to 28.45% (+CoT SFT) to 7.57% (+CoT Safety SFT) to 9.11% (+DPO) to 3.41% (+AW-DPO), while MMLU utility for AW-DPO (45.23%) is comparable to or better than the DPO baseline (41.45%). Similar patterns hold for Llama-3.2-3B (average ASR: 40.70% base -> 0.58% AW-DPO, utility 48.52%), Llama-3.1-8B (average ASR: 39.02% base -> 0.81% AW-DPO, utility 58.27%), and Mistral-7B-v0.3 (average ASR: 41.35% base -> 0.91% AW-DPO, utility 54.70%) (Table 1). The paper explicitly notes standard DPO can hurt utility (e.g., Mistral utility drops from 48.32% under CoT Safety SFT to 41.45% under +DPO) while AW-DPO recovers utility to 54.70% in that same setting. Against advanced alignment baselines on Llama-3.1-8B (Table 2): SAFECHAIN average ASR 25.80% (utility 44.88%), PP 4.55% (61.84%), STAIR 3.09% (70.38%), STAIR-DPO-3 1.33% (71.34%), vs. Ours(Instruct) 2.92% (65.29%) and Ours(Base) 0.81% (58.27%); the paper concedes STAIR-DPO-3 achieves higher safety and utility but attributes this to three rounds of iterative SFT+DPO (higher training cost) versus AW-DPO's single round. A dataset-transferability experiment (Table 3) shows an AW-DPO dataset built on Llama2-7B still yields strong safety/utility when used to train Llama3.2-3B, Llama3.1-8B, and Mistral-7B-V0.3 (e.g., average ASR 1.85%, 1.69%, 3.05% respectively), with some drop versus training on a model-matched dataset. An ablation on scaling factor alpha in {0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5} (Table 4) shows Base ASR stays flat at 1.14% across all values with utility varying 48.09%-49.43%, indicating low sensitivity to this hyperparameter in the tested range. Separately, evaluating reasoning-specialized models (Phi-4-Reasoning, Phi-4-Reasoning-Plus) shows they underperform on safety despite strong general reasoning benchmark scores, supporting the paper's claim that generic reasoning ability alone does not transfer to alignment robustness.
core_contribution: The paper's own contributions are threefold: (1) a causal-intervention study showing that current LLM safety alignment is superficial rather than reasoning-grounded — deactivating the top-10% of reasoning-critical attention heads (identified via per-head linear probing on the last-token hidden state, in the first 11 layers) collapses reasoning-task probing accuracy to near chance (~50%) while leaving alignment-task probing accuracy almost unchanged (near 100%); (2) a self-generated, open-sourced long-form Chain-of-Thought (CoT) fine-tuning dataset combining safety-focused and general-purpose instruction examples, with <think>...</think> reasoning traces, used for SFT that the paper shows beats vanilla/safety SFT baselines on both safety and utility; (3) Alignment-Weighted DPO (AW-DPO), a DPO variant that splits each response into a reasoning segment and a response segment (split at the </think> token), scores the harmfulness of each segment plus the full response with an LLM judge, computes per-segment preference weights w_reasoning and w_response from the harmfulness-score gaps between chosen/rejected candidates, and uses these weights to modulate the DPO loss contribution of each segment (Eq. 3-4), rather than treating the whole output as one unit as vanilla DPO does.
threat_model: Adversary submits jailbreak prompts that disguise harmful intent through indirect/deceptive phrasing (role-play, rhetorical manipulation, prompt obfuscation via ciphers or low-resource languages, formal-logic or code-injection framing, persuasion techniques, writing-style transformations) to bypass an LLM's safety alignment and elicit harmful content. The paper's claim is that these attacks succeed because alignment relies on shallow, pattern-matching refusal heuristics learned in early layers rather than a causal understanding of why content is harmful, so attacks that avoid the superficial trigger patterns slip past the refusal mechanism. AW-DPO's defense targets this failure mode at training time (post-training alignment), not at inference-time detection/filtering.
limitations: Self-acknowledged caveats found in the body text: (1) STAIR-DPO-3 outperforms AW-DPO on both safety and utility in Table 2, and the paper's efficiency argument (single-round vs. three-round training) is not accompanied by wall-clock/compute numbers. (2) The transferred AW-DPO dataset (Table 3) shows 'a slight drop in performance compared to training directly on the corresponding original dataset,' i.e., transferability is not free. (3) AW-DPO's utility on the Instruct variant (65.29%) is lower than PP (61.84%) is actually higher, but lower than STAIR/STAIR-DPO-3 (70.38%/71.34%), and the paper attributes open-source competitor advantages to 'proprietary datasets, extensive computational resources, or undisclosed hyperparameter settings' rather than quantifying the gap's cause. (4) The CoT dataset and full code are stated as released only 'upon acceptance of this work' (with an anonymous peer-review link provided for the CoT dataset), so full reproducibility artifacts were not yet public at the time of this text extraction. (5) The core causal-intervention (neuron-zeroing) result is demonstrated on two models (Llama-2-7B-Chat, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3) in the main text, with claimed but not extracted-in-detail generalization to 'other models' relegated to an appendix.

datasets_used: SorryBench (20 jailbreak attack types x 44 harmful-prompt categories, used to compute Attack Success Rate)
WildJailbreak (source of adversarial harmful prompts used to construct the AW-DPO preference dataset)
Self-constructed/self-generated long-form CoT dataset (safety-focused + general-purpose, released by the authors, with an anonymized peer-review link in the paper)
quantitative_results: Mistral-7B-v0.3 utility regression under vanilla DPO vs AW-DPO: 48.32% (CoT Safety SFT) -> 41.45% (+DPO) vs 54.70% (+AW-DPO)

Table 2 (Llama-3.1-8B) advanced-baseline comparison: SAFECHAIN avg ASR 25.80%/utility 44.88%; PP avg ASR 4.55%/utility 61.84%; STAIR avg ASR 3.09%/utility 70.38%; STAIR-DPO-3 avg ASR 1.33%/utility 71.34%; Ours(Instruct) avg ASR 2.92%/utility 65.29%; Ours(Base) avg ASR 0.81%/utility 58.27%

Table 3 transferability (AW-DPO dataset built on Llama2-7B applied to other models): Llama3.2-3B avg ASR 1.85%/utility 50.66%; Llama3.1-8B avg ASR 1.69%/utility 59.41%; Mistral-7B-V0.3 avg ASR 3.05%/utility 55.73%
Table 4 alpha ablation: Base ASR constant at 1.14% for alpha in {0.05,0.1,0.2,0.5}; average utility ranges 48.09%-49.43%

Qualitative error analysis on CoT-SFT model: the two targeted failure modes (correct reasoning+unsafe answer; incorrect reasoning+safe answer) account for ~15% of jailbreak failure cases, motivating AW-DPO's segment-level weighting

Causal intervention: reasoning-task probing accuracy drops from consistent >60% (later layers) to near chance (~50%) after deactivating top-10% reasoning-critical heads, while alignment-task probing accuracy stays near 100% before and after
baselines_compared: CoT Safety SFT (paper's own CoT dataset, no DPO)
Open-source aligned chat models (used for Figure 3 comparison, includes reasoning-specialized Phi-4-Reasoning / Phi-4-Reasoning-Plus)
